summary: Martin Fowler addresses the tension between Rails' deliberately opinionated, simple approach and enterprise software needs at RailsConf. He recounts DHH's emphatic refusal to add enterprise features like compound primary keys, comparing DHH's revolutionary mindset to Kent Beck's ability to question fundamental constraints. Fowler argues that while Rails is right to maintain its focused simplicity, the broader Ruby ecosystem can and should fill enterprise gaps through tools like rBatis. He concludes that 'Enterprise Rails' may be an oxymoron, but 'Enterprise Ruby' is viable and valuable, warning that Rails' dominance shouldn't mislead people into thinking Ruby itself is unsuitable for enterprise work.
key_points: Rails' 'opinionated software' philosophy means deliberately refusing to support patterns it doesn't like, such as compound primary keys
DHH and Kent Beck share a rare ability to question constraints others take for granted and blow them away with 'intellectual dynamite'
The distinction between Rails (narrow, opinionated) and Ruby (broad, flexible) allows enterprise needs to be met without compromising Rails' simplicity
Ruby's post-modern nature makes it ideal enterprise glue, embracing rather than hiding from messy software ecosystems
Rails' visibility creates a risk that its opinionated stance will be mistaken for Ruby's limitations
money_quotes: Here is a solid example of what makes Rails 'opinionated software'. In the Rails mindset, life is much simpler if you keep your tables isomorphic to your objects.
For either of them, if you present them with a constrained world, they'll look at constraints we take for granted, consider them to be unessential, and create a world without them.
I don't have that quality, I tend to try to work within the constraints gradually pushing at them, while they just stick some intellectual dynamite under them and move on.
When you need data from a database that's run by a data management group and has run for a decade with compound keys, you can't just don a pair of cool sunglasses and blow the constraint away.
Rails is right, I think, to ignore the enterprisey world, but that doesn't mean that Ruby should.
Enterprise Rails may well be an oxymoron, but Enterprise Ruby is anything but.
key_insight: A framework can maintain opinionated simplicity without limiting its language ecosystem, allowing focused tools and broader solutions to coexist.
